text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil.
zh: 大家好。这里是英国广播公司英语教学栏目《六分钟英语》。我是尼尔。

text: And I'm Pippa. Are you good at saving money, Neil, or do you like to spend it? Well, actually a bit of both.
zh: 我是皮帕。尼尔，你擅长存钱吗，还是喜欢花钱呢？其实两者都有一点。

text: I like to spend money on nice things, but I also try to save mainly because I've got children.
zh: 我喜欢把钱花在好东西上，但也会尽量存钱，主要是因为我有孩子。

text: How about you? Yeah, I'm the same. I like to save money, or I try to, for the future.
zh: 你呢？我也差不多。我喜欢存钱，或者说我尽量为将来存钱。

text: But I also do spend it. I don't count every penny that I spend and save every single penny that I earn.
zh: 但我也会花钱。我不会把花的每一分钱都算得清清楚楚，也不会把赚的每一分钱都存起来。

text: Hmm. Well, whether you're a saver or a spender, being in debt is common in the UK.
zh: 嗯，不管你是存钱族还是消费族，在英国负债都很常见。

text: Debt refers to money a person has borrowed to buy something
zh: 债务是指一个人为购买某样东西而借的钱，

text: and which they have to pay back, usually to a bank, a credit card company or another person.
zh: 并且必须偿还，通常是还给银行、信用卡公司或其他人。

text: Many people avoid talking about debt, but it affects us all. In 2025,
zh: 很多人避而不谈债务，但它影响着我们每一个人。2025年，

text: over 1 ,000 people contacted the UK Citizens Advice Bureau every single day of the year for help with their debt
zh: 英国公民咨询局每天都有超过1000人打来电话寻求债务方面的帮助，

text: and when debt gets out of control it causes stress and worry.
zh: 而当债务失控时，就会带来压力和焦虑。

text: BBC Radio 4 programme Thinking Aloud interviewed one young man Jason about his debt.
zh: BBC广播四台的节目《Thinking Aloud》采访了一位名叫杰森的年轻人，谈到他的债务问题。

text: Whatever jobs there are aren't enough to provide for a family. Sometimes you need to take out loans.
zh: 现有的工作根本不足以养家糊口，有时候你不得不去借贷。

text: I've done it a few times. You can't ever pay it back.
zh: 我借过好几次，根本还不完。

text: You can't see a way out other than winning the lottery or something. I'd like to think I could clear them all one day, even if it means like five years paying them off.
zh: 除了中彩票之类的，你根本看不到出路。我倒希望有朝一日能把债务全部还清，哪怕要花五年时间去还。

text: Jason uses three phrases – payback, pay off and clear debt –
zh: 杰森用了三个短语——payback、pay off 和 clear debt——

text: all of which mean the same thing, to give back the money you've borrowed.
zh: 它们意思相同，都是指偿还你所借的钱。

text: In this episode, we'll hear more about living with debt by learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 在这一集里，我们将通过学习一些实用的新单词和短语，进一步了解负债生活。

text: And remember, you'll find all the vocabulary used, plus a quiz and worksheet, on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 记住，你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期用到的所有词汇，还有配套测验和练习表。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Pippa. According to debt support group The Money Charity,
zh: 不过首先，我有个问题要问你，皮帕。根据债务支持机构“货币慈善会”（The Money Charity）的数据，

text: roughly how much is the average British adult in debt through credit cards, overdrafts and personal loans?
zh: 英国成年人平均通过信用卡、透支和个人贷款欠下多少债务？
是a）2200英镑，b）4200英镑，还是c）6200英镑？

text: I'm not sure. I'll say £2 ,200. £500. OK, well we will find out the answer later in the programme.
zh: 我不确定，我猜是2200英镑。500镑？好吧，我们节目稍后会揭晓答案。

text: Jason's story features in a new book by sociologist Ryan Davey.
zh: 杰森的故事出现在社会学家瑞安·戴维（Ryan Davey）的一本新书中。

text: Ryan spent months living in a low -income housing estate which he gave the fictional name Woldham.
zh: 瑞安花了数月时间住在一个低收入住宅区，他给这个地方起了个虚构的名字：沃尔德姆（Woldham）。

text: He talked with residents and listened to their money worries. Here, Ryan explains more to BBC Radio 4's Thinking Aloud.
zh: 他和居民交谈，倾听他们对金钱的担忧。在这里，瑞安向BBC广播四台的《Thinking Aloud》做了更多解释。

text: My approach was to let people know that I was interested in learning about their lives and how they were making ends meet.
zh: 我的方法是让人们知道，我对了解他们的生活以及他们如何维持生计很感兴趣。

text: So I did some interviews with residents and I paid attention to where debt came up in everyday conversations.
zh: 于是我采访了一些居民，并留意债务在日常对话中出现的情况。

text: For many people in Walden, Jason included, debt was an ordinary feature of daily life
zh: 对沃尔德姆的许多人来说，包括杰森在内，负债是日常生活中很平常的一部分，

text: and beyond that being in arrears, so being behind with one or more monthly payment commitment was part of daily life.
zh: 不仅如此，拖欠——也就是有一项或多项每月还款义务逾期未还——也是日常生活的一部分。

text: The people of Walden were making ends meet.
zh: 沃尔德姆的居民们都在勉强维持生计。

text: To make ends meet means having just enough money to pay for basic living expenses like food, bills and rent.
zh: “maintain ends meet”（收支相抵）指的是刚好有足够的钱来支付基本生活开支，比如食物、账单和房租。

text: Ryan found that many residents were in arrears, a phrase meaning to still owe money that should have already been repaid.
zh: 瑞安发现许多居民都处于“arrears”（拖欠）状态，这个词指的是仍欠着本该已经还清的钱。

text: For most residents, debt was a normal part of daily life.
zh: 对大多数居民来说，负债是日常生活中很正常的一部分。

text: With a regular income, debt can be managed,
zh: 只要有稳定的收入，债务是可以管理的，

text: but for those who are unemployed or on low incomes it can cause serious distress.
zh: 但对那些失业或收入低的人来说，债务可能带来严重的困扰。

text: Here, Ryan discusses how the residents he met felt about their debt with BBC Radio 4's Thinking Aloud.
zh: 在这里，瑞安向BBC广播四台的《Thinking Aloud》谈到他所遇到的居民对自己债务的感受。

text: Jason actually fluctuated between wanting to clear all of his debts on the one hand and on the other,
zh: 杰森其实在两种想法之间摇摆不定：一方面想把所有债务都还清，另一方面，

text: what he described as living on the never -never and actually questioning the supposed moral obligation to pay his debts.
zh: 他形容自己是“活在永不还清的分期付款里”（living on the never-never），并质疑所谓还债的道德义务是否真的存在。

text: Over the months that I knew him, the financial strain on him and his partner increased.
zh: 在我认识他的这几个月里，他和伴侣所承受的经济压力越来越大。

text: They missed some of their bills, their internet was disconnected. Jason was living on the never -never –
zh: 他们漏交了一些账单，网络也被断掉了。杰森当时正过着“the never-never”的生活——

text: an informal phrase for buying the things you need by making regular small payments over a long time.
zh: 这是一个非正式短语，指靠长期分期支付小额款项来购买所需物品。

text: It's called the never -never because it seems the debt will never be repaid. Jason experienced financial strain –
zh: 之所以叫“the never-never”，是因为这债务似乎永远也还不完。杰森经历了“financial strain”（经济压力）——

text: emotional stress caused by a lack of money to meet his basic needs or to repay his Debt is a serious issue.
zh: 这是指因为没有足够的钱满足基本需求或偿还债务而产生的情绪压力。债务是一个严重的问题。

text: It affects many people and and there are support groups who can help if you need it. OK, Neil, what was the answer to your question?
zh: 它影响着许多人，如果你需要帮助，也有支持团体可以提供援助。好了，尼尔，你那个问题的答案是什么？

text: I asked what the average amount of debt a British adult has through credit cards, overdrafts and personal loans.
zh: 我之前问的是，英国成年人通过信用卡、透支和个人贷款平均负债多少。

text: I said it was around £2 ,200. I'm afraid that's not the right answer.
zh: 我说大概是2200英镑。恐怕这个答案不对。

text: In fact, it's £4 ,232.
zh: 实际上是4232英镑。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with debt.
zh: 好，我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇，先从debt开始——

text: – money a person has borrowed and needs to give back.
zh: 指一个人所借的钱，需要偿还。

text: The phrases to pay back, to pay off and to clear a debt all mean to give back money you have borrowed.
zh: to pay back、to pay off 和 to clear a debt 这三个短语都表示偿还你所借的钱。

text: If someone is making ends meet, they have just enough money to pay for basic living expenses.
zh: 如果一个人make ends meet（收支相抵），那就是他有刚好足够的钱来支付基本生活开支。

text: And if they are in arrears, they still owe money that should have been repaid already.
zh: 如果他们in arrears（拖欠），那就是他们仍欠着本该已经还清的钱。

text: If you buy something on the never -never, you buy it by making small, regular payments over a long period.
zh: 如果你buy something on the never-never（分期购买），那就是通过长期支付小额、定期的款项来买这件东西。

text: And finally, financial strain is emotional stress caused by a lack of money to meet your basic needs.
zh: 最后，financial strain（经济压力）是指因缺钱而无法满足基本需求所带来的情绪压力。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you'd like to know how debt is spelled, and all the other words from this episode,
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了，不过如果你想知道debt这个词怎么拼写，以及本期出现的所有其他单词，

text: visit our website bbclearningenglish .com to find a full vocabulary list. See you again soon, but for now, it's goodbye. Bye.
zh: 欢迎访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com，查看完整的词汇表。我们下次再见，现在先说再见啦。拜拜。
